lines.target: When Paris was liberated by the Allies in 1945, a sign appeared in Paris shop windows: ENGLISH SPOKEN, AMERICAN UNDERSTOOD. Although the story may be apocryphal, it illustrates fairly well the popular attitudes to a question that still provokes enormous, if not always useful, debate.
lines.base: Cuando París fue liberada por los Aliados en 1945, un letrero apareció en los escaparates de las tiendas parisienses: *ENGLISH SPOKEN, AMERICAN UNDERSTOOD*. Aunque la historia pueda ser apócrifa, ilustra bastante bien las actitudes populares hacia una cuestión que todavía provoca un enorme debate, si no siempre útil.

lines.target: A language is a social organism — the tool of the society that creates it; as society changes, so those changes are reflected in the language. When that language is applied to describing a totally new world, it is obvious that the changes will be radical. In his famous book *«The American Language»*, H.L. Mencken declared: *«When the first American colonists had perforce to invent americanisms, if only to describe the unfamiliar landscape, weather, flora and fauna confronting them»*.
lines.base: Una lengua es un organismo social — la herramienta de la sociedad que la crea; a medida que la sociedad cambia, esos cambios se reflejan en la lengua. Cuando esa lengua se aplica a describir un mundo totalmente nuevo, es obvio que los cambios serán radicales. En su famoso libro *«El lenguaje americano»*, H.L. Mencken declaró: *«Cuando los primeros colonos americanos se vieron obligados a inventar americanismos, si solo era para describir el paisaje, el clima, la flora y la fauna no familiares que se encontraban»*.

lines.target: Mencken's book was published in 1919; its very title proposed that America had its own separate language (although the phrase *«the American language»* was first used by Congress in 1837). It also suggested that the language would, by a process of reverse osmosis, return to influence its creators, the British.
lines.base: El libro de Mencken se publicó en 1919; su mismo título proponía que América tenía su propio idioma separado (aunque la frase *«el lenguaje americano»* fue usada por primera vez por el Congreso en 1837). También sugirió que el idioma, por un proceso de ósmosis inversa, volvería a influir en sus creadores, los británicos.

lines.target: The Englishman, whether he knows it or not, is talking and writing more and more American English. In a few years it will probably be impossible for an Englishman to speak, or even to write, without using americanisms, whether consciously or unconsciously. The influence of 125 million people, all headed in one direction, is simply too strong to be resisted by any minority, however resolute.
lines.base: El inglés, ya sea que lo sepa o no, habla y escribe cada vez más en inglés americano. En unos pocos años, probablemente será imposible que un inglés hable, o incluso escriba, sin usar americanismos, ya sea consciente o inconscientemente. La influencia de 125 millones de personas, todas encaminadas en una dirección, es simplemente demasiado fuerte para ser resistida por cualquier minoría, por más resuelta que sea.

lines.target: (Those lines were written in 1930; the number of people mentioned by Mencken has since doubled.) What Mencken calls the American approach is characterized by energy: the energy of a new country and a new society that is restless, inventive and somewhat rootless. In the burst of energy that followed the creation of their Republic, the new Americans wanted to sever their links with Britain in every possible way. A new language was an obvious vehicle for declaring independence and shaping nationalism. Thomas Jefferson wrote about the differences between the two countries and said that *«audacious neology can alone give strength and copiousness to the language, and enable it to be the vehicle for new ideas»*. It is important that the language be an expression of national identity.
lines.base: (Esas líneas se escribieron en 1930; el número de personas mencionado por Mencken se ha doblado desde entonces.) Lo que Mencken menciona como el enfoque americano se caracteriza por la energía: la energía de un país nuevo y una sociedad nueva que es inquieta, inventiva y algo desarraigada. En el estallido de energía que siguió a la creación de su República, los nuevos americanos querían cortar sus lazos con Gran Bretaña de todas las maneras posibles. Un nuevo lenguaje era un vehículo obvio para declarar la independencia y moldear el nacionalismo. Thomas Jefferson escribió sobre las diferencias entre los dos países y dijo que *«la neología audaz puede solo dar fuerza y copiosidad al lenguaje, y permitirle que sea el vehículo para nuevas ideas»*. Es importante que el lenguaje sea una expresión de la identidad nacional.

lines.target: So we have seen revolutionary energy, the desire to create new words and retain old structures, and a desire to see a separation between the old language and the language of the new country. However, the influence of the media and the desire to find a *lingua franca* are reducing these differences still further; and although it would be possible to fill lesson after lesson in this book with slang, dialect and cant usage, it would only serve to highlight the fact that such usage is becoming increasingly marginal and that American English has established its identity without breaking totally with its British counterpart.
lines.base: Así que hemos visto energía revolucionaria, el deseo de crear nuevas palabras y conservar estructuras antiguas, y un deseo de ver una separación entre el antiguo lenguaje y el lenguaje del nuevo país. Sin embargo, la influencia de los medios de comunicación y el deseo de encontrar una *lengua franca* están reduciendo estas diferencias aún más; y aunque sería posible llenar lección tras lección en este libro con jerga, dialecto y uso de argot, solo serviría para resaltar el hecho de que tal uso se está volviendo cada vez más marginal y que el inglés americano ha establecido su identidad sin romper completamente con su contraparte británica.

lines.target: Character differences between the two peoples can (which is why a French visitor to the United States can often feel less «foreign» than a Briton in it — or because of — a common language). As Mr. Marckwardt says: *«the most striking feature of American English innovations is their close correspondence to the temperament and life styles of the people who developed them»*. American English, therefore, and not the American language.
lines.base: Las diferencias de carácter entre los dos pueblos pueden (es por lo que un visitante francés a los Estados Unidos puede a menudo sentirse menos «extranjero» que un británico en él — o a causa de — un lenguaje común). Como dice el Sr. Marckwardt: *«la característica más llamativa de las innovaciones del inglés americano es su estrecha correspondencia con el temperamento y los estilos de vida de la gente que las desarrolló»*. Inglés Americano, por lo tanto, y no el lenguaje americano.

gap_candidates.target: A language is a social organism — the tool of the society that creates it; as society changes, so those changes are reflected in the language. When that language is applied to describing a totally new world, it is obvious that the changes will be radical. In his famous book *«The American Language»*, H.L. Mencken declared: *«When the first American colonists had perforce to invent americanisms, if only to describe the unfamiliar landscape, weather, flora and fauna confronting them»*.

Mencken's book was published in 1919; its very title proposed that America had its own separate language (although the phrase *«the American language»* was first used by Congress in 1837). It also suggested that the language would, by a process of reverse osmosis, return to influence its creators, the British.

(Those lines were written in 1930; the number of people mentioned by Mencken has since doubled.) What Mencken calls the American approach is characterized by energy: the energy of a new country and a new society that is restless, inventive and somewhat rootless. In the burst of energy that followed the creation of their Republic, the new Americans wanted to sever their links with Britain in every possible way. A new language was an obvious vehicle for declaring independence and shaping nationalism. Thomas Jefferson wrote about the differences between the two countries and said that *«audacious neology can alone give strength and copiousness to the language, and enable it to be the vehicle for new ideas»*. It is important that the language be an expression of national identity.

So we have seen revolutionary energy, the desire to create new words and retain old structures, and a desire to see a separation between the old language and the language of the new country. However, the influence of the media and the desire to find a *lingua franca* are reducing these differences still further; and although it would be possible to fill lesson after lesson in this book with slang, dialect and cant usage, it would only serve to highlight the fact that such usage is becoming increasingly marginal and that American English has established its identity without breaking totally with its British counterpart.

Character differences between the two peoples can (which is why a French visitor to the United States can often feel less «foreign» than a Briton in it — or because of — a common language). As Mr. Marckwardt says: *«the most striking feature of American English innovations is their close correspondence to the temperament and life styles of the people who developed them»*. American English, therefore, and not the American language.
